name: Cova de Son Vila
starting_point: Leave the Ma-2200 road (connecting the Palma-Sa Pobla motorway with Pollensa) at Km 44.7
equipment: Helmet and lighting only, unless descending into the cave through the shaft, which requires approximately 20 m of rope
description: A cave in Pollensa visited since prehistoric times for water collection, as evidenced by pottery found inside. Features a shield-shaped entrance and a main chamber reaching 8 m depth, with well-decorated formations on the left side. Natural light entering from multiple openings creates a special atmosphere. Classified as a site of historic-archaeological and socio-economic interest.
full_description:
Like many other caves on the island of Mallorca, the Cova de Son Vila was visited since prehistoric times by people to collect water from its interior, as evidenced by the pottery found inside the cave. Today the interior of the cave remains dry, which is likely because the stream of water that used to descend the hillside and enter the cave through various openings has changed its course.

A path that starts from one of the streets of the housing development allows us to access the cave, ending right at its entrance. This path personally confused me for a long time, as I initially thought the construction of the path was due to an attempt at mining exploitation of the cave, but once I scrutinized its interior, everything indicates that no work has ever been carried out inside the cave, so perhaps it was intended to add an extra attraction to the housing development.

The cave entrance is oriented 245° N in the shape of a shield, being approximately one meter wide while the height does not exceed one meter and twenty centimeters. About seven meters above the main entrance, there is another smaller opening in the form of a shaft measuring approximately thirty by seventy centimeters, which seven or eight meters below connects with the cave in a small room.

Access to the cave is made by descending carefully to a sort of artificially constructed stone landing (photo 1), which is approximately one meter fifty below, where on the left side a fall would plunge us to the bottom of the great chamber that comprises this cave, which is eight meters deep.

Just behind a flowstone formation is the access path through a narrow corridor, on whose left side, in the direction of our travel, interesting concretions can be observed.

A small room opens to our right before reaching the bottom of the main chamber, which we will access through a small ramp. Once inside and if we look up at the ceiling, we can see how outside light filters through the shaft entrance, dimly illuminating the place.

Returning from the visit to the small room, we will descend to the bottom of the cavity, from where practically the entire chamber can be observed. Noteworthy is the natural light entering from outside through both the main entrance and some other small openings, giving the place a certain quality that will make us feel we are in a special place. As we advance through the chamber, we will immediately notice that the entire left side is quite well decorated with formations, which is not the case with the right side.

Another small recess, accessed by crossing a narrow crawlway, is found at the end of the cave and serves as a dormitory for some bat specimens.

The Cova de Son Vila is classified as a site of historic-archaeological interest, as well as socio-economic interest. J. A. Encinas S. Relación inventarial de subterráneos naturales de las Illes Baleares. 2006. Ediciones JAES.
